Facts.text: Battle at Andernach: King Otto and Hermann of Swabia defeat Eberhard of Franconia and Giselbert of Lotharingia
Hisham II is appointed Caliph of Cordoba
Storm flood ravages Zeeland county
The battle of Largs: Scots defeat the Norwegians on the Firth of Clyde, Scotland, returning the Western Isles to Scotland and ending 500 years of Viking invasions
English cardinal Thomas Wolsey makes European plan
Venice and Turkey signs peace
Spanish army occupies, plunders and destroys Mechelen
Hans Lippershey applies for patent for first known early telescope in the Netherlands
English North American colony of Connecticut passes law against Quakers
Spanish king Carlos II appoints Philip van Anjou as heir to the throne
Russian and Austrian army evacuates Berlin [NS=10/13]
Maagden House opens in Amsterdam
Baptist Missionary Society forms in London
Tula (leader slave uprising) sentence to death in Curacao
Duke of York & Russians capture Alkmaar in Netherlands
Britain mobilizes to protect against French invasion
NY Anti-Slavery Society organized
Battle of Gonzales fought between Texan settlers and Mexican forces. First engagement of the Texas revolution

The pasilalinic-sympathetic compass(a contraption built to test the pseudo-scientific hypothesis that snails create a permanent telepathic link when they mate) is demonstrated but proves to be a fake.
Austrian law forbids Jews from owning land
Former US VP John C Breckinridge flees Kentucky
Battle of Saltville, Virginia
J Osterhoudt patents tin can with key opener
Italy annexes Rome & Papal States; Rome made Italian capital
Morgan State University founded
Phileas Fogg sets out on his journey as depicted inJules Verne's "Around the World in Eighty Days"
First Pan American Conference, held in Washington, D.C.
In Colorado, Nicholas Creede strikes it rich in silver during the last great silver boom of the American Old West.
Cheniere Caminada hurricane (Great October Storm) - 3rd worst hurricane in US history kills 1,800 in Louisiana (one of the 1st to be classified as a category 4 storm)
1st cartoon comic strip is printed in a newspaper
Boer Republic of Orange Free State mobilizes militia in preparation for joint attack, with the Transvaal Republic, against British Colonies in South Africa (Second Boer War)
First Royal Navy submarine launched at Barrow-in-Furness

German General Lothar von Trotha issues order to exterminate native Herero and Namaqua peoples of German South West Africa (present day Namibia); 1st genocide of the 20th Century, kills 65,000 Herero and 10,000 of the Namaqua tribe
White Sox left-hander Doc White's streak of 45 consecutive MLB scoreless innings is snapped by the New York Highlanders in Chicago; White Sox win, 7-1 at South Side Park III
First Rugby Union match is played at the English RFU owned Twickenham ground; Harlequins beat Richmond, 14-10
1st 2 aircraft collision (Milan Italy)
Henry Wijnmalen flies to 2,800m altitude (world record)
Pitching in his 3rd start in 5 days, Philadelphia Phillies right handerGrover Cleveland Alexanderrecords his 20th century MLB record 16th shutout of year, a 2-0 win v Boston Braves
Zoological Society of San Diego founded, in San Diego, California
First edition of the Dutch newspaper "Volkskrant" (The People's Paper) is published
Cincinnati Reds and Pittsburgh Pirates play first and only MLB triple header in the 20th century; Reds win the first two games, 13-4 and 7-3; Pirates avoid the sweep in the finale, 6-0
The Chinese sign an agreement with the Russo-Asiatic Bank, largely French and Russian controlled, which will oversee the Chinese Eastern Railway, but give local supervision to China
Chicago White Sox backstop Ray Schalk becomes the first and only MLB catcher to make a putout at all bases; White Sox beat Indians 7-4
British occuping army leaves Constantinople
League of Nations approves protocols of Geneva
Bert Gibb kicks 9 singles as Hamilton Tigers beat Montreal AAA Winged Wheelers 12-10 in Montreal in Canadian Inter-provincial Rugby Football Union opening round
"Prelature of the Holy Cross and the Work of God", known as Opus Dei, founded by Catholic priest Josemaría Escrivá in Madrid, Spain
Washington Redskins (as Boston Braves) play franchise's debut NFL game; lose 14-0 to Brooklyn Dodgers at Braves Field
NY Hayden Planetarium, 4th in US, opens
Amsterdam's Calvinist Churches reject Nazism
First alcohol power plant forms in Atchison, Kansas
French franc devalued, relatively late and after Britain (1931) and the US (1933)
Prototype for the Ilyushin Il-2, world's most-produced military airplane designed by Sergey Ilyushin, takes it first flight. Over 36,000 made for Soviet airforce in WWII.
17 German aircraft shot down over England
British Council receives Royal Charter
6 Paris synagogues are bombed by Gestapo
Germans launch attack on Moscow
RMS Queen Mary, carrying thousands of US troops, slices cruiser HMS Curacao in half, killing 239
WWII: British Royal Navy boards floundering German U-boat U-559 retrieving abandoned Enigma code machine before it sinks; 2 British sailors go down with the sub
Japanese troops leave Kolombangara, Solomon Island
New York Yankees sweep 14th MLB doubleheader of year, beating St. Louis Browns, 5-1 & 7-6 at Yankee Stadium
Polish resistance fighters capitulate in the Warsaw Uprising after an estimated 250,000 people are killed
US B-17's drops pamphlets on Walcheren
1st network soap opera "Faraway Hill" begins broadcasting on DuMont Television Network
NY Yankees catcherYogi Berrahits the first pinch-hit home run in Baseball World Series history off Ralph Branca in the 7th inning of a 9-8 loss to the Brooklyn Dodgers in Game 3
Revised International Telecommunication Convention adopted

For first time in 41 years pennant races in both MLB leagues are decided on the final day of the season; (AL) Yankees upend rival Boston Red Sox, 5-3; (NL) Dodgers beat the Phillies, 9-7 for 97-57 record; Cardinals 96-58
USSR recognizes People's Republic of China
Bob Shaw of Chicago Cardinals sets NFL record with 5 TD receptions in 55-13 win against Baltimore Colts; Cardinals quarterback Jim Hardy tosses 6 touchdown passes
1st Netherland TV broadcast (Toverspiegel)

Don Liddle beats Bob Lemon 7-4 as the New York Giants complete an unlikely World Series sweep of the powerful Cleveland Indians; Cleveland season record of 111-43 sets American League mark for regular season wins
Former French possession of Chandernagore made part of West Bengal, India
"Alfred Hitchcock Presents" premieres on CBS television in the US
Chiva Stoica becomes premier of Romania
WHTN (now WOWK) TV ch 13 in Huntington-Charleston, WV (CBS) begins
1st atomic power clock exhibited-NYC
New volcanic island appears off Fayal Island Azores
Guinea (French Guinea) gains independence from France (National Day)
Musical show "Valmouth" 1st produced in London
USSR performs nuclear test at Novaya Zemlya USSR
Rod Serling's anthology series "The Twilight Zone" premieres on CBS-TV
American medical drama series "Ben Casey", starring Vince Edwards and Sam Jaffe, premieres on ABC-TV
USSR performs 250 kilo-ton nuclear test at Novaya Zemlya, USSR
WETA TV channel 26 in Washington, D.C. (PBS) begins broadcasting
WHRO TV channel 15 in Hampton-Norfolk, Vitginia (PBS) begins broadcasting
San Francisco Giants beat rival LA Dodgers, 8-7 in 4 hours 18 minutes in Game 2 NL tie-break series; longest 9-inning game in MLB history; Giants win series, 2-1
US performs atmospheric nuclear test at Johnston Island
NY Yankees pitcherMel Stottlemyrewins his 20th game of the season
PopePaul VInamed MR Perey bishop's helper of New Orleans
Australian Robert 'Nat' Young puts on a dominating performance to win the World Surfing Championship at Ocean Beach, San Diego; Joyce Hoffman of Capistrano Beach, California takes women's title
All Grateful Dead members arrested by narcotic agents for possession of marijuana in San Francisco, California
Groundbreaking begins on Veteran's Stadium in Philadelphia
Mexico City police fire on protesting students, 300-500 killed
Redwood National Park established to preserve the tallest trees on earth, by an act of US Congress with 58,000 acres (later extended)
Republic Guinea forms (day of republic)
Seattle Pilots' last game in Seattle; crash to 98th season loss, 3-1 to Oakland in front of just 5,473; move to Milwaukee as the Brewers next season
US performs underground nuclear test at Amchitka Island Aleutians
14 members of the Wichita State University Football team as well as 17 administrators and supporters are killed in a plane crash in the Rocky Mountains
Homing pigeon averages a record 133 kph in a 1,100 km race in Australia
Aeroflot Il-18 crashes near Black Sea resort of Sochi, kills 105
Bill Stoneman of Montreal pitches his 2nd no-hitter, beating Mets, 7-0
Danish population votes for membership of the European Common Market
Montreal Expos' pitcher Bill Stoneman no-hits the New York Mets, 7 - 0 at Parc Jarry; first MLB no-hitter ever pitched in Canada
Ron Johnson becomes 1st NY Giant to score 4 TDs (vs Philadelphia)
Four Ulster Volunteer Force members killed after a bomb they are carrying prematurely explodes in Farrenlester, near Coleraine
The Ulster Volunteer Force kill 7 civilians in a series of attacks across Northern Ireland; 6 were Catholic civilians and 1 was a Protestant civilian
Pakistani general Zia ul-Haq bans all opposition
Syrians and Palestinians exchange fire in East Beirut, killing 1,300
Michael Myers (D-Pa) is the first representative expelled in over 100 years after being caught in the ABSCAM sting operation
17-year-old gelding Behavin Jerry, the oldest winning racehorse in modern times, sets record for most career starts by a thoroughbred, 307
Ali Chamenei elected president of Iran
Bomb attack in Tehran, kills 60, injures 700
Carl Yastrezemski's last at bat
Green Bay Packers erupt for NFL record 49 points in the first half (35 in the second quarter) to clobber the Tampa Bay Buccaneers, 55-14 at Tampa Stadium
Wide receiver Art Monk begins NFL streak of 183 consecutive games with a reception in the Washington Redskins' 37-35 win over the LA Raiders
Due to a strike by MLB umpires, first 4 NLCS games played with replacement umpires; Game 1: Chicago Cubs beat SD Padres, 13-0
Papa John's Pizza is founded by John Schnatter in Jeffersonville, Indiana; it would go on to become the fourth-largest pizza delivery restaurant chain in the United States
Richard Miller becomes 1st (former) FBI agent to be charged with espionage
Three cosmonauts return after a record 237 days in orbit

A Mayor Richard Caliguiri led coalition of public and private groups committed to keeping the struggling Pittsburgh Pirates in town for at least 5 years reach agreement to buy the baseball team for $22 million
Detroit 1st baseman Darrell Evans hits home run #40 in Tigers' 4-2 win over Toronto Blue Jays; first to record 40 x HRs in a MLB season in both Leagues
Astros starter Mike Scott finishes MLB regular season with 306 strikeouts with 8 in a 2-1 win in San Francisco; 3rd NL pitcher to reach 300 in a season
Gelindo Bordin of Italy wins the men's marathon in 2:10:32 at the Seoul Olympics; beats world champion Douglas Wakiihuri of Kenya by 15 seconds
In a season ending 7-5 win in St. Louis, NY Mets' outfielder Kevin McReynolds establishes a MLB record swiping 21 bases without being caught stealing during the season
Mike Tysonwrecks furniture in his mansion in Bernardsville, NJ, during a domestic dispute
Minnesota Twins total 3,030,672 in attendance at Hubert H Humphrey Metrodome; first AL club ever to break 3 million in a MLB season
New Zealand horseman Mark Todd wins individual eventing gold medal aboard Charisma at the Seoul Olympics; his 2nd consecutive gold after winning in 1984
Pakistan's Supreme Court orders free elections
South Korean light middleweight boxer Park Si-hun is controversially awarded the Seoul Olympic gold medal after appearing well beaten by future 4 weight class world champion Roy Jones Jr
XXIV Summer Olympic Games close at the Seoul Olympic Stadium
TV true life drama "A Cry for Help: The Tracey Thurman Story" premieres on NBC in the US
Chinese plane explodes, about 100 die
Radio Berlin International's final transmission (links to Deutsche Welles of West Germany); final song is "The End" by Doors
US Senate votes 90-9 to confirm David Souter's appointment to Supreme Court
WW2 Allied nations cede any remaining rights as occupiers of Germany
Toronto Blue Jays clinch AL East title with a 6-5 win v California Angels; become first sport franchise in history to draw 4 million fans in one season
The Carandiru Massacre takes place after a riot in the Carandiru Penitentiary in São Paulo, Brazil
VP Itamar Franco becomes President of Brazil

Pakistan cricket batsmen Inzamam-ul-Haq & Mushtaq Ahmed put on 57 runs in 8 overs for highest last-wicket partnership ever to win a Test, as the home team beats Australia by 1 wicket in the 1st Test in Karachi
Pakistan defeat Australia by one wicket in Karachi Test
The Bruins beat the LA Kings, 6-5 in Boston to extend their NHL opening game unbeaten streak to 11-straight years (9-0-2)
With his team struggling at 28 for 3, Australian cricket batsman Steve Waugh smashes a brilliant 157 to guide the tourists to an innings & 99 run win over Pakistan in the 1st Test in Rawalpindi

#21 University of Alabama football team beats #3 Florida, 40-39 in overtime to snap the Gators' 30-game home win streak; future Seattle Seahawks running back Shaun Alexander crosses for 4 touchdowns for the Crimson Tide

Atlanta Thrashers play first regular-season game in franchise history, a 4-1 loss to the visiting NJ Devils, before a crowd of 18,545 at Philips Arena; Kelly Buchberger scores first goal in Thrashers history
The Beltway sniper attacks begin, extending over three weeks
American Samoa joins the North American Numbering Plan
Montreal Expos earn the last win in the franchise's MLB history, beating New York Mets, 6 - 3 at Shea Stadium; Brad Wilkerson hits the Expos' final home run in 9th inning, his 32nd of the year
NFL plays first regular season game outside the United States when the Arizona Cardinals defeat the San Francisco 49ers, 31-14 in Mexico City, Mexico
The Ethan Allen tour boat capsizes on Lake George in Upstate New York, killing twenty people
Charles Carl Roberts murders five schoolgirls in a shooting incident at an Amish school in Nickel Mines, Pennsylvania; he then commits suicide.
The economic crisis in Ireland is considered to be the driving force behind the largest migration of Irish people to London in 20 years, it is reported today
Sultan of EgyptSaladincaptures Jerusalem from the Crusaders
KingHenry VIIof England invades France
French explorerJacques Cartierfirst visits the Iroquois settlement of Hochelaga (later Montreal)
Conquest of Kazan by Russian TsarIvan the Terrible
French KingLouis XIIIdeclared an adult at 13
Last Emperor of the Chinese Ming dynasty,Chongzhen, comes to the throne after death of his brother Emperor Tianqi
After 5 years at seaCharles Darwinreturns to England aboard the HMS Beagle
US Mormon leaderBrigham Youngarrested for bigamy
Beatrix Potter's "The Tale of Peter Rabbit" is published by Frederick Warne & Co. in London
Canadian world heavyweight boxing championTommy BurnsKOs American challenger 'Fireman' Jim Flynn in 15 rounds to retain his title in Los Angeles, California
Cleveland Naps and future Baseball Hall of Fame pitcher Addie Joss hurls a classic perfect game, beatingEd Walshand the Chicago White Sox, 1-0
Gopal Krishna Gokhale, at invitation ofGandhi, arrives in South Africa on a 26-day tour; he also visits Tolstoy Farm
US PresidentWoodrow Wilsonhas a stroke, leaving him partially paralyzed
New York Yankees outfielderBabe Ruthhits then record 59th HR in 7-6 win over former club Boston Red Sox at Polo Grounds, NYC
Josephine Bakerfirst performs in Paris in La Revue Nègre at the Théâtre des Champs-Élysées
PopePius XIencyclical On economic crisis
Eugene O'Neill's comedy play "Ah, Wilderness!" premieres in NYC
FDRvisits Grand Coulee Dam construction site in Washington State
Future Baseball Hall of Fame pitcherBob Fellerstrikes out record 18 Detroit Tigers; his Cleveland Indians still lose 4-1 at Cleveland Stadium
1st strip ofCharlie Brown, "Li'l Folks", later "Peanuts", byCharles M. Schulzpublished in seven nationwide papers
West German ChancellorKonrad Adenauercondemns western grain shipments to USSR
Philadelphia'sAlex Johnson-Bobby Wine-Tony Taylor-Vic Power combine for Phillies' 3rd triple play of the season (tying MLB record) in 4-3 win v Cincinnati Reds
Los Angeles Dodgers pitcherSandy Koufaxwins his 27th game of the season, 6-3 v Philadelphia Phillies; clinches Dodgers 3rd National League pennant in 4 years
Thurgood Marshallsworn in as 1st black Supreme Court Justice
Detroit general manager Jim Campbell gives controversialBilly Martina 2-year deal (1971-72) to manage the Tigers at a well above MLB average annual salary of $65,000
Future Baseball Hall of Fame right fielderHank Aaronhits his final home run as a member of the Atlanta Braves, in a 13-0 drubbing of the Cincinnati Reds; Aaron's 733rd career HR on his last NL at bat
Austrian Ferrari driverNiki Laudafinishes 4th in the US Grand Prix East at Watkins Glen to clinch his second Formula 1 World Drivers Championship; Englishman James Hunt wins the race

Dusty Baker hits his 30th homer of the season against Astros' J.R. Richard; Dodgers become the first team in MLB history to have 4 players hit 30 or more HRs; joinsSteve Garvey(33), Reggie Smith (32) and Ron Cey (30)
NY Yankees win 3rd straight AL East title beating Boston Red Sox, 5-4 in a dramatic single playoff game;Ron Guidrywins #25 aided by Bucky Dent's 3-run homer
PopeJohn Paul IIvisits New York City, and at the U.N. denounces all concentration camps and torture

38-year-oldMuhammad Alicomes out of a two-year retirement to challenge undefeated world heavyweight championLarry Holmesat Caesars Palace, Las Vegas. Ali is pounded unmercifully for ten rounds before his corner throws in the towel.
The Portland Building, designed byMichael Graves, considered the 1st postmodern office tower opens in Portland, Oregon
Russian party leaderGorbachevvisits Paris (his first trip abroad)
New York Mets'Dwight Goodenbecomes first pitcher to collect 200 strikeouts in each of his first 3 seasons when he records 7 in 8-2 win v Pittsburgh Pirates
Sikhs attempt to assassinate Indian Prime MinisterRajiv Gandhi
Future world heavyweight boxing championLennox Lewis, representing Canada, wins super-heavyweight gold medal at the Seoul Olympics; beats AmericanRiddick Boweby 2nd round TKO
NHL #1 draft pickEric Lindrosrejects Quebec Nordiques' offer of a 10 year, $50m contract
Steffi Grafbeats Magdalena Maleeva of Bulgaria 6-2, 6-2 in the Volkswagen Cup in Leipzig to become the youngest woman to win 500 professional tennis matches
Legendary Miami head coachDon Shulabeats Cincinnati head coach and his son David in the first-ever NFL meeting between father and son; Dolphins defeat the Bengals, 23-7
British bandOasisrelease their second studio album "(What's the Story) Morning Glory?", sells record 347,000 copies 1st week
Boston'sRay Bourquebecomes highest goal-scoring defenseman in NHL history; 386th career goal in Bruins' 3-1 win v Carolina Hurricanes moves him 1 ahead ofPaul Coffey
American sitcom "Scrubs", starringZach Braffas Dr. "J.D." Dorian, premieres on NBC
Cubs right fielderSammy Sosabecomes first player in MLB history to total 60 home runs in three seasons; Chicago slugger connects off Reds starter Lance Davis to reach milestone in 5-4 loss
Jeff Kent becomes all-time home run leader for MLB 2nd basemen when he hits 2 in Astros' 9-3 win v Rockies; 302 overall HR to breakRyne Sandberg's major league record established in 1997
President Roh Moo-hyun of South Korea walks across the Military Demarcation Line into North Korea on his way to the second Inter-Korean Summit with North Korean leaderKim Jong Il
"Stan LeeDay" declared by County of Los Angeles and the City of Long Beach
George Washingtontransmits the proposed constitutional amendments (United States Bill of Rights) to the states for ratification
"The Bridge on the River Kwai", directed by David Lean and starringWilliam Holdenand Alec Guinness, is released (Academy Awards Best Picture 1958)
NATO states it will support US military strikes against Afghanistan in retaliation for the 9/11 terrorist attacks
Isabella of Aragon, Spanish heir of the Catholic Monarchs and Queen consort of Portugal (1497-98) had Jews expelled from Portugal, born in Dueñas, Castile (d. 1498)
Isabella of Naples, Duchess of Milan (1489-1494), born in Naples, Italy Naples, Kingdom of Naples (now Italy) (d. 1524)
Saint Charles Borromeo, Italian cardinal and Archbishop of Milan (1564-84), born in Arona, Milan, Italy (d. 1584)
Jacob Louys, Flemish engraver, born in Antwerp (d. 1668)
Andreas Gryphius, German lyric poet and dramatist, born in Glogau, Silesia (d. 1664)
François-Timoléon de Choisy, French writer (Quatre dialogues sur l'immortalité de l'âme), born in Paris (d. 1724)
Guillaume Poitevin, French composer and teacher, born in Boulbon, France (d. 1706)
František Tůma, Czech composer, born in Kostelec nad Orlicí, Kingdom of Bohemia (d. 1774)
Leopold Widhalm, Austrian luthier, born in Horn, Austria (d. 1776)
Franz Schneider, Austrian organist and composer, baptized in Pulkau, Austria (d. 1812)
Samuel Story, Dutch rear admiral (Battle at Kamperduin), born in Maasbommel, Dutch Republic (d. 1811)
Jacob van Strij, Dutch cartoonist and graphic artist, born in Dordrecht, Dutch Republic (d. 1815)
Józef Javurek, Polish-Russian pianist, composer, and educator, born in Benešov, Kingdom of Bohemia (d. 1840)
William Carr Beresford, 1st Viscount Beresford, British general and politician, born in Bedgebury, Kent, England (d. 1854)
William W. Bibb, American politician, Senator for Georgia, 1st governor of Alabama state and territory (1817-1820), born in  Amelia County, Virginia (d. 1820)
Charles Albert, King of Sardinia (1831-49), born in Turin, Italy (d. 1849)
Anton Emil Titl, Austrian composer, conductor, and kapellmeister, born in Nedvědice, Margraviate of Moravia, Austria-Hungary (d. 1882)
Gunnar Wennerberg, Swedish poet and composer, born in Lidköping, Sweden (d. 1901)
George Washington Getty, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Washington, D.C. (d. 1901)
Alexander P. Stewart, American Lieutenant General (Confederate Army), born in Rogersville, Hawkins County, Tennessee (d. 1908)
Jan Kappeyne van de Coppello, Prime Minister of the Netherlands (1877-79), born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1895)
Edmund Jackson Davis, American lawyer, soldier, and Brigadier General (Union Army), born in St. Augustine, Florida (d. 1883)
Charles Floquet, French statesman and 44th Prime Minister of France, born in Saint-Jean-Pied-de-Port, France (d. 1896)
Edward Burnett Tylor, English anthropologist (Primitive Culture), born in London (d. 1917)
Julius von Sachs, German botanist and naturalist, born in Breslau, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1897)
William Corby, American Catholic priest and President of the University of Notre Dame, born in Detroit, Michigan (d. 1897)
Louis-Antoine Ranvier, French anatomist and historian, born in Lyon, France (d. 1922)
Hans Thoma, German painter, born in Bernau, Black Forest (d. 1924)
Ferdinand Foch, French Allied military commander and marshal of France during WW I, born in Tarbes, France (d. 1929)
Patrick Geddes, Scottish biologist and town planner who coined the term conurbation, born in Ballater, Aberdeenshire, Scotland (d. 1932)
Marthinus Theunis Steyn, South African politician and President of Orange-Free state (1896-1902), born in Winburg, Orange Free State (d. 1916)
Charles Ricketts, British artist and illustrator, born in Geneva, Switzerland (d. 1931)
Cordell Hull, American Secretary of State (1933-44) who established United Nation (Nobel 1945), born in Olympus, Tennessee (d. 1955)
Martha Brookes Hutcheson, American landscape architect, born in New York City (d. 1959)
Sir Pelham Warner, English cricket batsman, tour manager and administrator (15 Tests; MCC chairman of selectors), born in The Hall, Port of Spain, Trinidad (d. 1963)
Stephen Warfield Gambrill, American politician (Member of the U.S. House of Representatives from Maryland), born in Savage, Maryland (d. 1938)
Henry Février, French composer, born in Paris (d. 1957)
William C. Fownes Jr, American golfer and administrator (US Amateur C'ship 1910; President US Golf Ass'n 1926-27), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 1950)
François Conrad Schlumberger, French physicist, born in Guebwiller, Upper Rhine (d. 1936)
Wilfrid Wilson, British Georgian poet (Flannan Isle), born in Hexham, Northumberland, England (d. 1962)
Wallace Stevens, American poet (Ideas of Order), born in Reading, Pennsylvania (d. 1955)
George Alexander Russell, American organist, composer and the first Frick Professor of Music for Princeton University, born in Franklin, Tennessee (d. 1953)
Ferdinand Barlow, French composer (Mam'zelle Prud'homme), born in Mulhouse, France (d. 1951)
Boris Shaposhnikov, Russian military commander, born in Zlatoust, Ufa Governorate Russian Empire (d. 1945)
Ruth Bryan Owen, American politician (first woman appointed as a U.S. ambassador, Denmark 1933-36), born in Jacksonville, Illinois (d. 1954)
Pierre Balledux, Dutch actor, theatre director (The Hague Players), and resistance fighter, born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1976)
Margaret Chung, Chinese-American physician who was the first known Chinese-American woman to earn a medical degree, born in Santa Barbara, California (d. 1959)
H. V. Porter, American basketball coach and administrator (created fan-shaped backboard; moulded basketball, rules films), born in Manito, Illinois (d. 1975)
Leroy Shield [Shields], American pianist, composer (Laurel & Hardy; Our Gang; Union Pacific Suite), and orchestra leader, born in Waseca, Minnesota (d. 1962)
Fritz Eberhard, [Helmut von Rauschenplat], German journalist, anti-fascist war resistor, and co-signer of modern constitution, born in Dresden, German Empire (d. 1982)
Louis Lebeer, Belgian art historian, engraver, and academic, born in Mechelen, Belgium (d. 1986)
Bud Abbott, American comedian (Abbott & Costello), born in Asbury Park, New Jersey (d. 1974)
Barton Yarborough, American radio and screen actor (One Man's Family; Dragnet), born in Goldthwaite, Texas (d. 1951)

Charles Stark "Doc" Draper, American Scientist and Engineer who headed the team that invented the Apollo Guidance Computer and inertial navigation technology for aircraft, space vehicles, and submarines, born in Windsor, Missouri (d. 1987)
Christopher Clarkson, British test pilot, born in Dunsfold, Surrey (d. 1994)
Roy Campbell, South African poet (Flowering Rifle), born in Durban, South Africa (d. 1957)
Leopold Figl, Austrian politician (13th Chancellor of Austria), born in Rust, Michelhausen, Austria-Hungary (d. 1965)
Dermot Boyle, Marshal of the Royal Air Force, born in Rathdowney, County Laois, Ireland (d. 1993)
Godfrey Baseley, British radio executive, born in Alverchurch, Worchestershire, England (d. 1997)
Lal Bahadur Shastri, Indian politician and Prime Minister of India (1964-66), born in Mughalsarai, British India (d. 1966)
John Humphreys Whitfield, British scholar of Italian language & literature, born in Wednesbury, England (d. 1995)
Alexander Robertus Todd, British organic chemist (Nobel Prize 1957), born in Glasgow (d. 1997)
Víctor Paz Estenssoro, Bolivian politician, President of Bolivia (1952–56, 1960–64, 1985–89), born in Tarija, Bolivia (d. 2001)
Jack Finney, American sci-fi and thriller author (The Body Snatchers, Time and Again), born in Milwaukee, Wisconsin (d. 1995)
Stuart Rose, British designer (British Post Office), born in London (d. 1993)
Eric Charles Twelves Wilson, English British Army officer (awarded Victoria Cross), born in Sandown, Isle of Wight, England (d. 2008)
Gene Benson, American baseball outfielder (Negro League All-Star 1940, 45-46), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 1999)
Karl Miller, German soccer defender (12 caps; Dresdner SC), born in Hamburg (d. 1967)
Vivian Ridler, British scholar and printer (Oxford University), born in Cardiff, Wales (d. 2009)
Albe Vidaković, Croatian composer, born in Subotica, Serbia (d. 1964)
Bengt Johansson, Finnish composer, born in Helsinki, Finland (d. 1989)
Bernarr Rainbow, English historian of music education, organist, and choir master, born in London (d. 1998)
Charles Drake [Ruppert], American actor (Harvey, Air Force, Glenn Miller Story), born in New York City (d. 1994)
Jack Parsons, American rocket scientist and Thelemite occultist, born in Los Angeles, California (d. 1952)
Robert Russell "Chubby" Wise, American bluegrass fiddler, born in Lake City, Florida (d. 1996)
George Siravo, American big band and session saxophonist, composer, arranger and conductor (Columbia Records), born in Staten Island, New York (d. 2000)
Christian de Duve, British cytologist and biochemist (Nobel 1974), born in Thames Ditton, Surrey (d. 2013)
Francis Jackson, British organist, composer (East Acklam; A Time of Fire), and music director (York Minster, 1946-82), born in Malton, Yorkshire, England (d. 2022)
William Marshall, American singer, bandleader and actor (State Fair, Pennsylvanians), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1994)
Shirley Clarke, American film director, born in New York City (d. 1997)
Giuseppe Colombo, Italian mathematician and engineer who studied Mercury, known as 'the grandfather of the flyby', born in Padua, Italy (d. 1984)[1]
James Dobson, American actor (Impulse, Jet Attack), born in Greeneville, Tennessee
Spec Shea, American baseball pitcher (MLB All Star, World Series 1947; NY Yankees, Washington Sens), born in Naugatuck, Connecticut (d. 2002)
William Cramond, Scottish psychiatrist and vice-chancellor (Stirling University), born in Aberdeen, Scotland (d. 2004)
Albert Scott Crossfield, American test pilot (XS-1), born in Berkeley, California (d. 2006)
Robert Runcie, Archbishop of Canterbury, born in Liverpool, England (d. 2000)
Otmar Mácha, Czech composer (Quattro Group), born in Mariánské Hory, Ostrava, Czechoslovakia (d. 2006)
Jack Dellal, British property investor and multi-millionaire (Allied Commercial Holdings), born in Chorlton-cum-Hardy, Manchester, England, (d. 2012)
Gilbert Simondon, Frenchphilosopher(On the Mode of Existence of Technical Objects), born in Saint-Étienne, France (d. 1989)
Alois Piňos, Czech composer, born in Vyskov, Czechoslovakia (d. 2008)
Brian Dillon, British lawyer and judge, Lord Justice of Appeal (1982-94), born in England (d. 2003)
